=== Iwase {{ill|Shikobuchi Shrine|ja|志子淵神社}} === <gallery> File:6-shicobuchi.JPG|Shikobuchi Shrine, main hall </gallery> Located in Kutsuki Iwase, Takashima, Shiga Prefecture. Shikobuchi-kami is revered as the "God of Rafting," and an annual ''Shikobuchi Kō'' religious gathering is held.
=== Nakano {{ill|Shishibuchi Shrine (Takashima, Azumi River Town, Nakano)|ja|思子淵神社 (高島市安曇川町中野)}} === <gallery> File:7-shicobuchi.JPG|Shishibuchi Shrine, approach path </gallery> Located in Azumi River Town, Nakano, Takashima, Shiga Prefecture.
== Other Shishibuchi Shrines == ; Kutsuki Miyamaebō {{ill|Shishibuchi Daimyōjin|ja|思子渕大明神}} : Enshrined within {{ill|Ninigi Shrine|ja|邇々杵神社}}. Located between the main halls of Ninigi Shrine and {{ill|Kawachi Shrine|ja|河内神社}}, three small shrines stand together. It is believed to have been relocated from another site.
; Katsuragawa Hosokawa {{ill|Shikobuchi Shrine|ja|シコブチ神社}} : An auxiliary shrine of the Hachiman Shrine, situated on a mountain slope along the right bank of the Azumi River. It is believed to have been relocated from another site.
; Katsuragawa Machii {{ill|Shishibuchi Shrine|ja|思子渕神社}} : Located north of the Machii village settlement along the left bank of the Katsuragawa River, on a mountain slope behind a temple.
; Ōhara Momoi {{ill|Shishibuchi Shrine|ja|思子淵神社}} : Located in {{ill|Ōhara, Kyoto|ja|大原 (京都市)}}, Momoi-chō, Sakyō-ku, Kyoto. The shrine and a stage hall stand on a steep slope along the left bank of the Momoi River. Revered by forestry workers, its torii gate is constructed from unstripped {{ill|Kitayama cedar|ja|北山杉}}. <gallery> File:思古淵神社.jpg|Shikobuchi Shrine </gallery>
; Ōhara Ōmi {{ill|Shishibuchi Shrine|ja|思子淵神社}} : Located in Ōhara Ōmi-chō, Sakyō-ku, Kyoto. Uniquely situated at the source of the Ōmi River, with a river flowing between its approach path and main hall. : Destroyed by Typhoon No. 18 in September 2013, it was rebuilt in April 2018. A discovered ridge plaque revealed that the previous shrine was originally founded in the Muromachi period (1386) and rebuilt in the Edo period (1725). (''Asahi Shimbun Digital'', May 12, 2018: "Rebuilding the Shrine in Kyoto's Hidden Village: A New Beginning")
; Ōhara Ogoshi {{ill|Shishibuchi Shrine|ja|思子淵神社}} : Located in Ōhara Ogoshi-chō, Sakyō-ku, Kyoto, on a mountain slope along the left bank of the Ashibiyadani River. The shrine building has collapsed, leaving only the lanterns.
== The Gawatarō Legend == Long ago, Shikobuchi Myōjin and his child were rafting down the Azumi River from Tsuzukigahara in Katsuragawa Umenoki when their raft suddenly stopped. Upon investigating, they discovered that a large rock in Kanayama-buchi had blocked the raft's movement. At the same time, Shikobuchi Myōjin's child had vanished. Shocked, Myōjin plunged his pole into the water and searched the depths, finding a kappa hiding at the bottom with the child. Myōjin immediately captured the kappa, rescued his child, and scolded the kappa harshly. However, when they reached ''Aka-kabe'' in Nakano, the kappa reappeared and disrupted the raft again. Enraged, Myōjin warned the kappa, "From now on, if you ever interfere with someone wearing a ''suge no minokasa'' (straw raincoat) and ''gama no kyahan'' (rush leggings) while carrying a ''kobushi'' pole, you will face severe punishment!" It is believed that Shikobuchi Myōjin was originally a local land deity. == See Also == * {{ill|Kappa|ja|河童}}
== External Links == * [http://www.pref.shiga.lg.jp/minwa/54/54-butai.html Tracing Shiga’s Landscape Through Folktales: "Shikobuchi-san"] (Includes audio) ** [http://www.pref.shiga.lg.jp/minwa/54/54-01.html Shikobuchi Shrine]

revision.text:
'''Seven Shikobuchi''' (七シコブチ) is a collective term for the seven major shrines dedicated to the deity '''Shikobuchi-kami''', a faith unique to the {{ill|Azumi River|ja|安曇川}} basin flowing through {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府}} and {{ill|Shiga Prefecture|ja|滋賀県}}. Legends of the ''Gawatarō'' ({{ill|Kappa|ja|河童}}) remain in all seven shrines. The name "Shikobuchi" is written in various forms, including 思子淵, 志古渕, and 信興淵.
== The Seven Shikobuchi Shrines == === Kuta {{ill|Shikobuchi Shrine|ja|思古渕社}} (Shikobuchi Jinja) === <gallery> File:1-shicobuchi.JPG|Shikobuchi Shrine, front view </gallery> Located in {{ill|Kuta|ja|久多}}, {{ill|Sakyō-ku|ja|左京区}}, {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都市}}, this shrine is home to the ''Hanagasa-odori'' dance, which is designated as an intangible cultural asset.
=== Ogawa {{ill|Shishibuchi Shrine|ja|思子淵神社}} === <gallery> File:2-shicobuchi.JPG|Shishibuchi Shrine, front view </gallery> Located in Kutsuki Ogawa, {{ill|Takashima|ja|高島市}}, Shiga Prefecture. A religious gathering called ''Shishibuchi Kō'' was held annually, where participants selected a host on a rotating basis, hung a sacred scroll inscribed with "Shishibuchi Shrine" in the alcove, made offerings, and prepared red rice for a communal meal. The shrine also possesses a copy of the ''Daihannya Sutra'', both designated as cultural assets.
=== Sakashita {{ill|Shishibuchi Daimyōjin|ja|思子渕大明神}} === <gallery> File:3-shicobuchi.JPG|Shishibuchi Daimyōjin, front view </gallery> Located in Katsuragawa Sakashita, {{ill|Ōtsu|ja|大津市}}, Shiga Prefecture. The shrine is a small sanctuary built atop a large boulder.
=== Bōmura {{ill|Shinkōbuchi Daimyōjin|ja|信興淵大明神}} === <gallery> File:4-shicobuchi.JPG|Katsuragawa Jinushi Shrine main hall & auxiliary shrine (right side) </gallery> Located in Katsuragawa Bōmura, Ōtsu, Shiga Prefecture. Enshrined as an auxiliary shrine within {{ill|Katsuragawa Jinushi Shrine|ja|葛川地主神社}}, it is associated with the founding of {{ill|Katsuragawa Myōō-in|ja|葛川明王院}}.
=== Umenoki {{ill|Shikobuchi Shrine|ja|志子渕神社}} === <gallery> File:5-shicobuchi.JPG|Shikobuchi Shrine </gallery> Located in Umenoki-chō, Ōtsu, Shiga Prefecture. Until recent times, its festival was held on the 7th day of the 10th lunar month.

; Katsuragawa Machii {{ill|Shishibuchi Shrine|ja|思子渕神社}} : Located north of the Machii village settlement along the left bank of the Katsuragawa River, on a mountain slope behind a temple.
; Ōhara Momoi {{ill|Shishibuchi Shrine|ja|思子淵神社}} : Located in {{ill|Ōhara, Kyoto|ja|大原 (京都市)}}, Momoi-chō, Sakyō-ku, Kyoto. The shrine and a stage hall stand on a steep slope along the left bank of the Momoi River. Revered by forestry workers, its torii gate is constructed from unstripped {{ill|Kitayama cedar|ja|北山杉}}. <gallery> File:思古淵神社.jpg|Shikobuchi Shrine </gallery>
; Ōhara Ōmi {{ill|Shishibuchi Shrine|ja|思子淵神社}} : Located in Ōhara Ōmi-chō, Sakyō-ku, Kyoto. Uniquely situated at the source of the Ōmi River, with a river flowing between its approach path and main hall. : Destroyed by Typhoon No. 18 in September 2013, it was rebuilt in April 2018. A discovered ridge plaque revealed that the previous shrine was originally founded in the Muromachi period (1386) and rebuilt in the Edo period (1725). Located between the main halls of Ninigi Shrine and {{ill|Kawachi Shrine|ja|河内神社|12=draft}}, three small shrines stand together. It is believed to have been relocated from another site.
; Katsuragawa Hosokawa {{ill|Shikobuchi Shrine|ja|シコブチ神社|12=draft}} : An auxiliary shrine of the Hachiman Shrine, situated on a mountain slope along the right bank of the Azumi River. It is believed to have been relocated from another site.
; Katsuragawa Machii {{ill|Shishibuchi Shrine|ja|思子渕神社|12=draft}} : Located north of the Machii village settlement along the left bank of the Katsuragawa River, on a mountain slope behind a temple.
; Ōhara Momoi {{ill|Shishibuchi Shrine|ja|思子淵神社|12=draft}} : Located in {{ill|Ōhara, Kyoto|ja|大原 (京都市)|12=draft}}, Momoi-chō, Sakyō-ku, Kyoto. The shrine and a stage hall stand on a steep slope along the left bank of the Momoi River. Revered by forestry workers, its torii gate is constructed from unstripped {{ill|Kitayama cedar|ja|北山杉|12=draft}}. <gallery> File:思古淵神社.jpg|Shikobuchi Shrine </gallery>
; Ōhara Ōmi {{ill|Shishibuchi Shrine|ja|思子淵神社|12=draft}} : Located in Ōhara Ōmi-chō, Sakyō-ku, Kyoto. Uniquely situated at the source of the Ōmi River, with a river flowing between its approach path and main hall. : Destroyed by Typhoon No. 18 in September 2013, it was rebuilt in April 2018. A discovered ridge plaque revealed that the previous shrine was originally founded in the Muromachi period (1386) and rebuilt in the Edo period (1725). (''Asahi Shimbun Digital'', May 12, 2018: "Rebuilding the Shrine in Kyoto's Hidden Village: A New Beginning")
; Ōhara Ogoshi {{ill|Shishibuchi Shrine|ja|思子淵神社|12=draft}} : Located in Ōhara Ogoshi-chō, Sakyō-ku, Kyoto, on a mountain slope along the left bank of the Ashibiyadani River. The shrine building has collapsed, leaving only the lanterns.
== The Gawatarō Legend == Long ago, Shikobuchi Myōjin and his child were rafting down the Azumi River from Tsuzukigahara in Katsuragawa Umenoki when their raft suddenly stopped. Upon investigating, they discovered that a large rock in Kanayama-buchi had blocked the raft's movement. At the same time, Shikobuchi Myōjin's child had vanished. Shocked, Myōjin plunged his pole into the water and searched the depths, finding a kappa hiding at the bottom with the child. Myōjin immediately captured the kappa, rescued his child, and scolded the kappa harshly. However, when they reached ''Aka-kabe'' in Nakano, the kappa reappeared and disrupted the raft again. Enraged, Myōjin warned the kappa, "From now on, if you ever interfere with someone wearing a ''suge no minokasa'' (straw raincoat) and ''gama no kyahan'' (rush leggings) while carrying a ''kobushi'' pole, you will face severe punishment!" It is believed that Shikobuchi Myōjin was originally a local land deity. == See Also == * {{ill|Kappa|ja|河童|12=draft}}
== External Links == * [http://www.pref.shiga.lg.jp/minwa/54/54-butai.html Tracing Shiga’s Landscape Through Folktales: "Shikobuchi-san"] (Includes audio) ** [http://www.pref.shiga.lg.jp/minwa/54/54-01.html Shikobuchi Shrine]

revision.comment: Text replacement - "↵↵↵↵" to " "
revision.text:
'''Seven Shikobuchi''' (七シコブチ) is a collective term for the seven major shrines dedicated to the deity '''Shikobuchi-kami''', a faith unique to the {{ill|Azumi River|ja|安曇川|12=draft}} basin flowing through {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府|12=draft}} and {{ill|Shiga Prefecture|ja|滋賀県|12=draft}}. Legends of the ''Gawatarō'' ({{ill|Kappa|ja|河童|12=draft}}) remain in all seven shrines. The name "Shikobuchi" is written in various forms, including 思子淵, 志古渕, and 信興淵.
== The Seven Shikobuchi Shrines == === Kuta {{ill|Shikobuchi Shrine|ja|思古渕社|12=draft}} (Shikobuchi Jinja) === <gallery> File:1-shicobuchi.JPG|Shikobuchi Shrine, front view </gallery> Located in {{ill|Kuta|ja|久多|12=draft}}, {{ill|Sakyō-ku|ja|左京区|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都市|12=draft}}, this shrine is home to the ''Hanagasa-odori'' dance, which is designated as an intangible cultural asset.
=== Ogawa {{ill|Shishibuchi Shrine|ja|思子淵神社|12=draft}} === <gallery> File:2-shicobuchi.JPG|Shishibuchi Shrine, front view </gallery> Located in Kutsuki Ogawa, {{ill|Takashima|ja|高島市|12=draft}}, Shiga Prefecture. A religious gathering called ''Shishibuchi Kō'' was held annually, where participants selected a host on a rotating basis, hung a sacred scroll inscribed with "Shishibuchi Shrine" in the alcove, made offerings, and prepared red rice for a communal meal. The shrine also possesses a copy of the ''Daihannya Sutra'', both designated as cultural assets.
=== Sakashita {{ill|Shishibuchi Daimyōjin|ja|思子渕大明神|12=draft}} === <gallery> File:3-shicobuchi.JPG|Shishibuchi Daimyōjin, front view </gallery> Located in Katsuragawa Sakashita, {{ill|Ōtsu|ja|大津市|12=draft}}, Shiga Prefecture. The shrine is a small sanctuary built atop a large boulder.
=== Bōmura {{ill|Shinkōbuchi Daimyōjin|ja|信興淵大明神|12=draft}} === <gallery> File:4-shicobuchi.JPG|Katsuragawa Jinushi Shrine main hall & auxiliary shrine (right side) </gallery> Located in Katsuragawa Bōmura, Ōtsu, Shiga Prefecture. Enshrined as an auxiliary shrine within {{ill|Katsuragawa Jinushi Shrine|ja|葛川地主神社|12=draft}}, it is associated with the founding of {{ill|Katsuragawa Myōō-in|ja|葛川明王院|12=draft}}.
=== Umenoki {{ill|Shikobuchi Shrine|ja|志子渕神社|12=draft}} === <gallery> File:5-shicobuchi.JPG|Shikobuchi Shrine </gallery> Located in Umenoki-chō, Ōtsu, Shiga Prefecture. Until recent times, its festival was held on the 7th day of the 10th lunar month.
=== Iwase {{ill|Shikobuchi Shrine|ja|志子淵神社|12=draft}} === <gallery> File:6-shicobuchi.JPG|Shikobuchi Shrine, main hall </gallery> Located in Kutsuki Iwase, Takashima, Shiga Prefecture. Shikobuchi-kami is revered as the "God of Rafting," and an annual ''Shikobuchi Kō'' religious gathering is held.
=== Nakano {{ill|Shishibuchi Shrine (Takashima, Azumi River Town, Nakano)|ja|思子淵神社 (高島市安曇川町中野)|12=draft}} === <gallery> File:7-shicobuchi.JPG|Shishibuchi Shrine, approach path </gallery> Located in Azumi River Town, Nakano, Takashima, Shiga Prefecture.
== Other Shishibuchi Shrines == ; Kutsuki Miyamaebō {{ill|Shishibuchi Daimyōjin|ja|思子渕大明神|12=draft}} : Enshrined within {{ill|Ninigi Shrine|ja|邇々杵神社|12=draft}}. Located between the main halls of Ninigi Shrine and {{ill|Kawachi Shrine|ja|河内神社|12=draft}}, three small shrines stand together. It is believed to have been relocated from another site.
; Katsuragawa Hosokawa {{ill|Shikobuchi Shrine|ja|シコブチ神社|12=draft}} : An auxiliary shrine of the Hachiman Shrine, situated on a mountain slope along the right bank of the Azumi River. It is believed to have been relocated from another site.
; Katsuragawa Machii {{ill|Shishibuchi Shrine|ja|思子渕神社|12=draft}} : Located north of the Machii village settlement along the left bank of the Katsuragawa River, on a mountain slope behind a temple.
; Ōhara Momoi {{ill|Shishibuchi Shrine|ja|思子淵神社|12=draft}} : Located in {{ill|Ōhara, Kyoto|ja|大原 (京都市)|12=draft}}, Momoi-chō, Sakyō-ku, Kyoto. The shrine and a stage hall stand on a steep slope along the left bank of the Momoi River. Revered by forestry workers, its torii gate is constructed from unstripped {{ill|Kitayama cedar|ja|北山杉|12=draft}}. <gallery> File:思古淵神社.jpg|Shikobuchi Shrine </gallery>
; Ōhara Ōmi {{ill|Shishibuchi Shrine|ja|思子淵神社|12=draft}} : Located in Ōhara Ōmi-chō, Sakyō-ku, Kyoto. Uniquely situated at the source of the Ōmi River, with a river flowing between its approach path and main hall. : Destroyed by Typhoon No. 18 in September 2013, it was rebuilt in April 2018. A discovered ridge plaque revealed that the previous shrine was originally founded in the Muromachi period (1386) and rebuilt in the Edo period (1725). (''Asahi Shimbun Digital'', May 12, 2018: "Rebuilding the Shrine in Kyoto's Hidden Village: A New Beginning")
; Ōhara Ogoshi {{ill|Shishibuchi Shrine|ja|思子淵神社|12=draft}} : Located in Ōhara Ogoshi-chō, Sakyō-ku, Kyoto, on a mountain slope along the left bank of the Ashibiyadani River. The shrine building has collapsed, leaving only the lanterns.
== The Gawatarō Legend == Long ago, Shikobuchi Myōjin and his child were rafting down the Azumi River from Tsuzukigahara in Katsuragawa Umenoki when their raft suddenly stopped. Upon investigating, they discovered that a large rock in Kanayama-buchi had blocked the raft's movement. At the same time, Shikobuchi Myōjin's child had vanished. Shocked, Myōjin plunged his pole into the water and searched the depths, finding a kappa hiding at the bottom with the child. Myōjin immediately captured the kappa, rescued his child, and scolded the kappa harshly. However, when they reached ''Aka-kabe'' in Nakano, the kappa reappeared and disrupted the raft again. Enraged, Myōjin warned the kappa, "From now on, if you ever interfere with someone wearing a ''suge no minokasa'' (straw raincoat) and ''gama no kyahan'' (rush leggings) while carrying a ''kobushi'' pole, you will face severe punishment!" It is believed that Shikobuchi Myōjin was originally a local land deity. == See Also == * {{ill|Kappa|ja|河童|12=draft}}
== External Links == * [http://www.pref.shiga.lg.jp/minwa/54/54-butai.html Tracing Shiga’s Landscape Through Folktales: "Shikobuchi-san"] (Includes audio) ** [http://www.pref.shiga.lg.jp/minwa/54/54-01.html Shikobuchi Shrine]

(''Asahi Shimbun Digital'', May 12, 2018: "Rebuilding the Shrine in Kyoto's Hidden Village: A New Beginning")
; Ōhara Ogoshi {{ill|Shishibuchi Shrine|ja|思子淵神社|12=draft}} : Located in Ōhara Ogoshi-chō, Sakyō-ku, Kyoto, on a mountain slope along the left bank of the Ashibiyadani River. The shrine building has collapsed, leaving only the lanterns.
== The Gawatarō Legend == Long ago, Shikobuchi Myōjin and his child were rafting down the Azumi River from Tsuzukigahara in Katsuragawa Umenoki when their raft suddenly stopped. Upon investigating, they discovered that a large rock in Kanayama-buchi had blocked the raft's movement. At the same time, Shikobuchi Myōjin's child had vanished. Shocked, Myōjin plunged his pole into the water and searched the depths, finding a kappa hiding at the bottom with the child. Myōjin immediately captured the kappa, rescued his child, and scolded the kappa harshly. However, when they reached ''Aka-kabe'' in Nakano, the kappa reappeared and disrupted the raft again. Enraged, Myōjin warned the kappa, "From now on, if you ever interfere with someone wearing a ''suge no minokasa'' (straw raincoat) and ''gama no kyahan'' (rush leggings) while carrying a ''kobushi'' pole, you will face severe punishment!" It is believed that Shikobuchi Myōjin was originally a local land deity. == See Also == * {{ill|Kappa|ja|河童|12=draft}}
== External Links == * [http://www.pref.shiga.lg.jp/minwa/54/54-butai.html Tracing Shiga’s Landscape Through Folktales: "Shikobuchi-san"] (Includes audio) ** [http://www.pref.shiga.lg.jp/minwa/54/54-01.html Shikobuchi Shrine]

revision.text:
'''Seven Shikobuchi''' (七シコブチ) is a collective term for the seven major shrines dedicated to the deity '''Shikobuchi-kami''', a faith unique to the {{ill|Azumi River|ja|安曇川|12=draft}} basin flowing through {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府|12=draft}} and {{ill|Shiga Prefecture|ja|滋賀県|12=draft}}. Legends of the ''Gawatarō'' ({{ill|Kappa|ja|河童|12=draft}}) remain in all seven shrines. The name "Shikobuchi" is written in various forms, including 思子淵, 志古渕, and 信興淵.
== The Seven Shikobuchi Shrines == === Kuta {{ill|Shikobuchi Shrine|ja|思古渕社|12=draft}} (Shikobuchi Jinja) === <gallery> File:1-shicobuchi.JPG|Shikobuchi Shrine, front view </gallery> Located in {{ill|Kuta|ja|久多|12=draft}}, {{ill|Sakyō-ku|ja|左京区|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都市|12=draft}}, this shrine is home to the ''Hanagasa-odori'' dance, which is designated as an intangible cultural asset.
=== Ogawa {{ill|Shishibuchi Shrine|ja|思子淵神社|12=draft}} === <gallery> File:2-shicobuchi.JPG|Shishibuchi Shrine, front view </gallery> Located in Kutsuki Ogawa, {{ill|Takashima|ja|高島市|12=draft}}, Shiga Prefecture. A religious gathering called ''Shishibuchi Kō'' was held annually, where participants selected a host on a rotating basis, hung a sacred scroll inscribed with "Shishibuchi Shrine" in the alcove, made offerings, and prepared red rice for a communal meal. The shrine also possesses a copy of the ''Daihannya Sutra'', both designated as cultural assets.
=== Sakashita {{ill|Shishibuchi Daimyōjin|ja|思子渕大明神|12=draft}} === <gallery> File:3-shicobuchi.JPG|Shishibuchi Daimyōjin, front view </gallery> Located in Katsuragawa Sakashita, {{ill|Ōtsu|ja|大津市|12=draft}}, Shiga Prefecture. The shrine is a small sanctuary built atop a large boulder.
=== Bōmura {{ill|Shinkōbuchi Daimyōjin|ja|信興淵大明神|12=draft}} === <gallery> File:4-shicobuchi.JPG|Katsuragawa Jinushi Shrine main hall & auxiliary shrine (right side) </gallery> Located in Katsuragawa Bōmura, Ōtsu, Shiga Prefecture. Enshrined as an auxiliary shrine within {{ill|Katsuragawa Jinushi Shrine|ja|葛川地主神社|12=draft}}, it is associated with the founding of {{ill|Katsuragawa Myōō-in|ja|葛川明王院|12=draft}}.
=== Umenoki {{ill|Shikobuchi Shrine|ja|志子渕神社|12=draft}} === <gallery> File:5-shicobuchi.JPG|Shikobuchi Shrine </gallery> Located in Umenoki-chō, Ōtsu, Shiga Prefecture. Until recent times, its festival was held on the 7th day of the 10th lunar month.
=== Iwase {{ill|Shikobuchi Shrine|ja|志子淵神社|12=draft}} === <gallery> File:6-shicobuchi.JPG|Shikobuchi Shrine, main hall </gallery> Located in Kutsuki Iwase, Takashima, Shiga Prefecture. Shikobuchi-kami is revered as the "God of Rafting," and an annual ''Shikobuchi Kō'' religious gathering is held.
=== Nakano {{ill|Shishibuchi Shrine (Takashima, Azumi River Town, Nakano)|ja|思子淵神社 (高島市安曇川町中野)|12=draft}} === <gallery> File:7-shicobuchi.JPG|Shishibuchi Shrine, approach path </gallery> Located in Azumi River Town, Nakano, Takashima, Shiga Prefecture.
== Other Shishibuchi Shrines == ; Kutsuki Miyamaebō {{ill|Shishibuchi Daimyōjin|ja|思子渕大明神|12=draft}} : Enshrined within {{ill|Ninigi Shrine|ja|邇々杵神社|12=draft}}. Located between the main halls of Ninigi Shrine and {{ill|Kawachi Shrine|ja|河内神社|12=draft}}, three small shrines stand together. It is believed to have been relocated from another site.
; Katsuragawa Hosokawa {{ill|Shikobuchi Shrine|ja|シコブチ神社|12=draft}} : An auxiliary shrine of the Hachiman Shrine, situated on a mountain slope along the right bank of the Azumi River. It is believed to have been relocated from another site.
; Katsuragawa Machii {{ill|Shishibuchi Shrine|ja|思子渕神社|12=draft}} : Located north of the Machii village settlement along the left bank of the Katsuragawa River, on a mountain slope behind a temple.
; Ōhara Momoi {{ill|Shishibuchi Shrine|ja|思子淵神社|12=draft}} : Located in {{ill|Ōhara, Kyoto|ja|大原 (京都市)|12=draft}}, Momoi-chō, Sakyō-ku, Kyoto. The shrine and a stage hall stand on a steep slope along the left bank of the Momoi River. Revered by forestry workers, its torii gate is constructed from unstripped {{ill|Kitayama cedar|ja|北山杉|12=draft}}. <gallery> File:思古淵神社.jpg|Shikobuchi Shrine </gallery>
; Ōhara Ōmi {{ill|Shishibuchi Shrine|ja|思子淵神社|12=draft}} : Located in Ōhara Ōmi-chō, Sakyō-ku, Kyoto. Uniquely situated at the source of the Ōmi River, with a river flowing between its approach path and main hall. : Destroyed by Typhoon No. 18 in September 2013, it was rebuilt in April 2018. A discovered ridge plaque revealed that the previous shrine was originally founded in the Muromachi period (1386) and rebuilt in the Edo period (1725). (''Asahi Shimbun Digital'', May 12, 2018: "Rebuilding the Shrine in Kyoto's Hidden Village: A New Beginning")
; Ōhara Ogoshi {{ill|Shishibuchi Shrine|ja|思子淵神社|12=draft}} : Located in Ōhara Ogoshi-chō, Sakyō-ku, Kyoto, on a mountain slope along the left bank of the Ashibiyadani River. The shrine building has collapsed, leaving only the lanterns.
== The Gawatarō Legend == Long ago, Shikobuchi Myōjin and his child were rafting down the Azumi River from Tsuzukigahara in Katsuragawa Umenoki when their raft suddenly stopped. Upon investigating, they discovered that a large rock in Kanayama-buchi had blocked the raft's movement. At the same time, Shikobuchi Myōjin's child had vanished. Shocked, Myōjin plunged his pole into the water and searched the depths, finding a kappa hiding at the bottom with the child. Myōjin immediately captured the kappa, rescued his child, and scolded the kappa harshly. However, when they reached ''Aka-kabe'' in Nakano, the kappa reappeared and disrupted the raft again. Enraged, Myōjin warned the kappa, "From now on, if you ever interfere with someone wearing a ''suge no minokasa'' (straw raincoat) and ''gama no kyahan'' (rush leggings) while carrying a ''kobushi'' pole, you will face severe punishment!" It is believed that Shikobuchi Myōjin was originally a local land deity. == See Also == * {{ill|Kappa|ja|河童|12=draft}}
== External Links == * [http://www.pref.shiga.lg.jp/minwa/54/54-butai.html Tracing Shiga’s Landscape Through Folktales: "Shikobuchi-san"] (Includes audio) ** [http://www.pref.shiga.lg.jp/minwa/54/54-01.html Shikobuchi Shrine]

== External Links == * [http://www.pref.shiga.lg.jp/minwa/54/54-butai.html Tracing Shiga’s Landscape Through Folktales: "Shikobuchi-san"] (Includes audio) ** [http://www.pref.shiga.lg.jp/minwa/54/54-01.html Shikobuchi Shrine]

revision.comment: Adding [[Category:qq]][[sn:{{subst:PAGENAME}}]][[Category:qq]] using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
'''Seven Shikobuchi''' (七シコブチ) is a collective term for the seven major shrines dedicated to the deity '''Shikobuchi-kami''', a faith unique to the {{ill|Azumi River|ja|安曇川|12=draft}} basin flowing through {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府|12=draft}} and {{ill|Shiga Prefecture|ja|滋賀県|12=draft}}. Legends of the ''Gawatarō'' ({{ill|Kappa|ja|河童|12=draft}}) remain in all seven shrines. The name "Shikobuchi" is written in various forms, including 思子淵, 志古渕, and 信興淵.
== The Seven Shikobuchi Shrines == === Kuta {{ill|Shikobuchi Shrine|ja|思古渕社|12=draft}} (Shikobuchi Jinja) === <gallery> File:1-shicobuchi.JPG|Shikobuchi Shrine, front view </gallery> Located in {{ill|Kuta|ja|久多|12=draft}}, {{ill|Sakyō-ku|ja|左京区|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都市|12=draft}}, this shrine is home to the ''Hanagasa-odori'' dance, which is designated as an intangible cultural asset.
=== Ogawa {{ill|Shishibuchi Shrine|ja|思子淵神社|12=draft}} === <gallery> File:2-shicobuchi.JPG|Shishibuchi Shrine, front view </gallery> Located in Kutsuki Ogawa, {{ill|Takashima|ja|高島市|12=draft}}, Shiga Prefecture. A religious gathering called ''Shishibuchi Kō'' was held annually, where participants selected a host on a rotating basis, hung a sacred scroll inscribed with "Shishibuchi Shrine" in the alcove, made offerings, and prepared red rice for a communal meal. The shrine also possesses a copy of the ''Daihannya Sutra'', both designated as cultural assets.
=== Sakashita {{ill|Shishibuchi Daimyōjin|ja|思子渕大明神|12=draft}} === <gallery> File:3-shicobuchi.JPG|Shishibuchi Daimyōjin, front view </gallery> Located in Katsuragawa Sakashita, {{ill|Ōtsu|ja|大津市|12=draft}}, Shiga Prefecture. The shrine is a small sanctuary built atop a large boulder.
=== Bōmura {{ill|Shinkōbuchi Daimyōjin|ja|信興淵大明神|12=draft}} === <gallery> File:4-shicobuchi.JPG|Katsuragawa Jinushi Shrine main hall & auxiliary shrine (right side) </gallery> Located in Katsuragawa Bōmura, Ōtsu, Shiga Prefecture. Enshrined as an auxiliary shrine within {{ill|Katsuragawa Jinushi Shrine|ja|葛川地主神社|12=draft}}, it is associated with the founding of {{ill|Katsuragawa Myōō-in|ja|葛川明王院|12=draft}}.
=== Umenoki {{ill|Shikobuchi Shrine|ja|志子渕神社|12=draft}} === <gallery> File:5-shicobuchi.JPG|Shikobuchi Shrine </gallery> Located in Umenoki-chō, Ōtsu, Shiga Prefecture. Until recent times, its festival was held on the 7th day of the 10th lunar month.
=== Iwase {{ill|Shikobuchi Shrine|ja|志子淵神社|12=draft}} === <gallery> File:6-shicobuchi.JPG|Shikobuchi Shrine, main hall </gallery> Located in Kutsuki Iwase, Takashima, Shiga Prefecture. Shikobuchi-kami is revered as the "God of Rafting," and an annual ''Shikobuchi Kō'' religious gathering is held.
=== Nakano {{ill|Shishibuchi Shrine (Takashima, Azumi River Town, Nakano)|ja|思子淵神社 (高島市安曇川町中野)|12=draft}} === <gallery> File:7-shicobuchi.JPG|Shishibuchi Shrine, approach path </gallery> Located in Azumi River Town, Nakano, Takashima, Shiga Prefecture.
== Other Shishibuchi Shrines == ; Kutsuki Miyamaebō {{ill|Shishibuchi Daimyōjin|ja|思子渕大明神|12=draft}} : Enshrined within {{ill|Ninigi Shrine|ja|邇々杵神社|12=draft}}. Located between the main halls of Ninigi Shrine and {{ill|Kawachi Shrine|ja|河内神社|12=draft}}, three small shrines stand together. It is believed to have been relocated from another site.
; Katsuragawa Hosokawa {{ill|Shikobuchi Shrine|ja|シコブチ神社|12=draft}} : An auxiliary shrine of the Hachiman Shrine, situated on a mountain slope along the right bank of the Azumi River. It is believed to have been relocated from another site.
; Katsuragawa Machii {{ill|Shishibuchi Shrine|ja|思子渕神社|12=draft}} : Located north of the Machii village settlement along the left bank of the Katsuragawa River, on a mountain slope behind a temple.
; Ōhara Momoi {{ill|Shishibuchi Shrine|ja|思子淵神社|12=draft}} : Located in {{ill|Ōhara, Kyoto|ja|大原 (京都市)|12=draft}}, Momoi-chō, Sakyō-ku, Kyoto. The shrine and a stage hall stand on a steep slope along the left bank of the Momoi River. Revered by forestry workers, its torii gate is constructed from unstripped {{ill|Kitayama cedar|ja|北山杉|12=draft}}. <gallery> File:思古淵神社.jpg|Shikobuchi Shrine </gallery>
; Ōhara Ōmi {{ill|Shishibuchi Shrine|ja|思子淵神社|12=draft}} : Located in Ōhara Ōmi-chō, Sakyō-ku, Kyoto. Uniquely situated at the source of the Ōmi River, with a river flowing between its approach path and main hall. : Destroyed by Typhoon No. 18 in September 2013, it was rebuilt in April 2018. A discovered ridge plaque revealed that the previous shrine was originally founded in the Muromachi period (1386) and rebuilt in the Edo period (1725). (''Asahi Shimbun Digital'', May 12, 2018: "Rebuilding the Shrine in Kyoto's Hidden Village: A New Beginning")
; Ōhara Ogoshi {{ill|Shishibuchi Shrine|ja|思子淵神社|12=draft}} : Located in Ōhara Ogoshi-chō, Sakyō-ku, Kyoto, on a mountain slope along the left bank of the Ashibiyadani River. The shrine building has collapsed, leaving only the lanterns.
== The Gawatarō Legend == Long ago, Shikobuchi Myōjin and his child were rafting down the Azumi River from Tsuzukigahara in Katsuragawa Umenoki when their raft suddenly stopped. Upon investigating, they discovered that a large rock in Kanayama-buchi had blocked the raft's movement. At the same time, Shikobuchi Myōjin's child had vanished. Shocked, Myōjin plunged his pole into the water and searched the depths, finding a kappa hiding at the bottom with the child. Myōjin immediately captured the kappa, rescued his child, and scolded the kappa harshly. However, when they reached ''Aka-kabe'' in Nakano, the kappa reappeared and disrupted the raft again. Enraged, Myōjin warned the kappa, "From now on, if you ever interfere with someone wearing a ''suge no minokasa'' (straw raincoat) and ''gama no kyahan'' (rush leggings) while carrying a ''kobushi'' pole, you will face severe punishment!" It is believed that Shikobuchi Myōjin was originally a local land deity. == See Also == * {{ill|Kappa|ja|河童|12=draft}}
== External Links == * [http://www.pref.shiga.lg.jp/minwa/54/54-butai.html Tracing Shiga’s Landscape Through Folktales: "Shikobuchi-san"] (Includes audio) ** [http://www.pref.shiga.lg.jp/minwa/54/54-01.html Shikobuchi Shrine]

revision.comment: Text replacement - "↵↵↵↵↵↵↵↵↵↵↵↵↵↵↵↵↵↵↵↵" to " "
revision.text:
'''Seven Shikobuchi''' (七シコブチ) is a collective term for the seven major shrines dedicated to the deity '''Shikobuchi-kami''', a faith unique to the {{ill|Azumi River|ja|安曇川|qq=draft|qq=draft}} basin flowing through {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Shiga Prefecture|ja|滋賀県|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Legends of the ''Gawatarō'' ({{ill|Kappa|ja|河童|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) remain in all seven shrines. The name "Shikobuchi" is written in various forms, including 思子淵, 志古渕, and 信興淵.
== The Seven Shikobuchi Shrines == === Kuta {{ill|Shikobuchi Shrine|ja|思古渕社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Shikobuchi Jinja) === <gallery> File:1-shicobuchi.JPG|Shikobuchi Shrine, front view </gallery> Located in {{ill|Kuta|ja|久多|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Sakyō-ku|ja|左京区|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, this shrine is home to the ''Hanagasa-odori'' dance, which is designated as an intangible cultural asset.
=== Ogawa {{ill|Shishibuchi Shrine|ja|思子淵神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} === <gallery> File:2-shicobuchi.JPG|Shishibuchi Shrine, front view </gallery> Located in Kutsuki Ogawa, {{ill|Takashima|ja|高島市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Shiga Prefecture. A religious gathering called ''Shishibuchi Kō'' was held annually, where participants selected a host on a rotating basis, hung a sacred scroll inscribed with "Shishibuchi Shrine" in the alcove, made offerings, and prepared red rice for a communal meal. The shrine also possesses a copy of the ''Daihannya Sutra'', both designated as cultural assets.
=== Sakashita {{ill|Shishibuchi Daimyōjin|ja|思子渕大明神|qq=draft|qq=draft}} === <gallery> File:3-shicobuchi.JPG|Shishibuchi Daimyōjin, front view </gallery> Located in Katsuragawa Sakashita, {{ill|Ōtsu|ja|大津市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Shiga Prefecture. The shrine is a small sanctuary built atop a large boulder.
=== Bōmura {{ill|Shinkōbuchi Daimyōjin|ja|信興淵大明神|qq=draft|qq=draft}} === <gallery> File:4-shicobuchi.JPG|Katsuragawa Jinushi Shrine main hall & auxiliary shrine (right side) </gallery> Located in Katsuragawa Bōmura, Ōtsu, Shiga Prefecture. Enshrined as an auxiliary shrine within {{ill|Katsuragawa Jinushi Shrine|ja|葛川地主神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, it is associated with the founding of {{ill|Katsuragawa Myōō-in|ja|葛川明王院|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
=== Umenoki {{ill|Shikobuchi Shrine|ja|志子渕神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} === <gallery> File:5-shicobuchi.JPG|Shikobuchi Shrine </gallery> Located in Umenoki-chō, Ōtsu, Shiga Prefecture. Until recent times, its festival was held on the 7th day of the 10th lunar month.
=== Iwase {{ill|Shikobuchi Shrine|ja|志子淵神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} === <gallery> File:6-shicobuchi.JPG|Shikobuchi Shrine, main hall </gallery> Located in Kutsuki Iwase, Takashima, Shiga Prefecture. Shikobuchi-kami is revered as the "God of Rafting," and an annual ''Shikobuchi Kō'' religious gathering is held.
=== Nakano {{ill|Shishibuchi Shrine (Takashima, Azumi River Town, Nakano)|ja|思子淵神社 (高島市安曇川町中野)|qq=draft|qq=draft}} === <gallery> File:7-shicobuchi.JPG|Shishibuchi Shrine, approach path </gallery> Located in Azumi River Town, Nakano, Takashima, Shiga Prefecture.
== Other Shishibuchi Shrines == ; Kutsuki Miyamaebō {{ill|Shishibuchi Daimyōjin|ja|思子渕大明神|qq=draft|qq=draft}} : Enshrined within {{ill|Ninigi Shrine|ja|邇々杵神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Located between the main halls of Ninigi Shrine and {{ill|Kawachi Shrine|ja|河内神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, three small shrines stand together. It is believed to have been relocated from another site.
; Katsuragawa Hosokawa {{ill|Shikobuchi Shrine|ja|シコブチ神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} : An auxiliary shrine of the Hachiman Shrine, situated on a mountain slope along the right bank of the Azumi River. It is believed to have been relocated from another site.
; Katsuragawa Machii {{ill|Shishibuchi Shrine|ja|思子渕神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} : Located north of the Machii village settlement along the left bank of the Katsuragawa River, on a mountain slope behind a temple.
; Ōhara Momoi {{ill|Shishibuchi Shrine|ja|思子淵神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} : Located in {{ill|Ōhara, Kyoto|ja|大原 (京都市)|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Momoi-chō, Sakyō-ku, Kyoto. The shrine and a stage hall stand on a steep slope along the left bank of the Momoi River. Revered by forestry workers, its torii gate is constructed from unstripped {{ill|Kitayama cedar|ja|北山杉|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. <gallery> File:思古淵神社.jpg|Shikobuchi Shrine </gallery>
; Ōhara Ōmi {{ill|Shishibuchi Shrine|ja|思子淵神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} : Located in Ōhara Ōmi-chō, Sakyō-ku, Kyoto. Uniquely situated at the source of the Ōmi River, with a river flowing between its approach path and main hall. : Destroyed by Typhoon No. 18 in September 2013, it was rebuilt in April 2018. A discovered ridge plaque revealed that the previous shrine was originally founded in the Muromachi period (1386) and rebuilt in the Edo period (1725). (''Asahi Shimbun Digital'', May 12, 2018: "Rebuilding the Shrine in Kyoto's Hidden Village: A New Beginning")
; Ōhara Ogoshi {{ill|Shishibuchi Shrine|ja|思子淵神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} : Located in Ōhara Ogoshi-chō, Sakyō-ku, Kyoto, on a mountain slope along the left bank of the Ashibiyadani River. The shrine building has collapsed, leaving only the lanterns.
== The Gawatarō Legend == Long ago, Shikobuchi Myōjin and his child were rafting down the Azumi River from Tsuzukigahara in Katsuragawa Umenoki when their raft suddenly stopped. Upon investigating, they discovered that a large rock in Kanayama-buchi had blocked the raft's movement. At the same time, Shikobuchi Myōjin's child had vanished. Shocked, Myōjin plunged his pole into the water and searched the depths, finding a kappa hiding at the bottom with the child. Myōjin immediately captured the kappa, rescued his child, and scolded the kappa harshly. However, when they reached ''Aka-kabe'' in Nakano, the kappa reappeared and disrupted the raft again. Enraged, Myōjin warned the kappa, "From now on, if you ever interfere with someone wearing a ''suge no minokasa'' (straw raincoat) and ''gama no kyahan'' (rush leggings) while carrying a ''kobushi'' pole, you will face severe punishment!" It is believed that Shikobuchi Myōjin was originally a local land deity. == See Also == * {{ill|Kappa|ja|河童|qq=draft|qq=draft}}
== External Links == * [http://www.pref.shiga.lg.jp/minwa/54/54-butai.html Tracing Shiga’s Landscape Through Folktales: "Shikobuchi-san"] (Includes audio) ** [http://www.pref.shiga.lg.jp/minwa/54/54-01.html Shikobuchi Shrine]

revision.comment: 28 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:七シコブチ up to rev 99974881
revision.text:
｢'''七シコブチ'''｣とは、[[京都府]]と[[滋賀県]]を流れる[[安曇川]]流域固有の信仰神である｢'''しこぶち神'''｣を祀る主要な7社の総称。7社にはいずれもガワタロウ（[[河童]]）伝説が残る。「しこぶち」は、思子淵、志古渕、信興淵などと表記される。
== 七シコブチ == === 久多 [[思古渕社|思古渕社(志古淵神社)]] === <gallery> ファイル:1-shicobuchi.JPG|志古渕神社 正面 </gallery> 京都府[[京都市]][[左京区]][[久多|久多中の町]]に鎮座。花笠踊は無形文化財に指定されている。 === 小川 思子淵神社 === <gallery> ファイル:2-shicobuchi.JPG|思子淵神社 正面 </gallery> 滋賀県[[高島市]]朽木小川に鎮座。毎年輪番に宿を決めて集まり、床の間に神軸（「思子淵神社」と書かれている）を掛けてお供えをし、赤飯を炊いて会食する思子淵講があった。大般若経を有し、いずれも文化財に指定されている。 === 坂下 思子渕大明神 === <gallery> ファイル:3-shicobuchi.JPG|思子渕大明神 正面 </gallery> 滋賀県[[大津市]]葛川坂下に町鎮座。大岩の上に鎮座する小さな祠に祀られている。 === 坊村 信興淵大明神 === <gallery> ファイル:4-shicobuchi.JPG|葛川地主神社本殿・境内社（向かって右） </gallery> 滋賀県大津市葛川坊村町に鎮座。地主神社の境内社として祀られている。[[葛川明王院]]の創始にまつわる伝承がある。 === 梅の木 志子渕神社 === <gallery> ファイル:5-shicobuchi.JPG|志子渕神社 </gallery> 滋賀県大津市梅の木町に鎮座。近年まで、陰暦十月七日が祭日であった。 === 岩瀬 [[志子淵神社]] === <gallery> ファイル:6-shicobuchi.JPG|志子淵神社 本殿 </gallery> 滋賀県高島市朽木岩瀬に鎮座。志子淵神を「筏流しの神様」として祀り、毎年志子淵講が行われる。 === 中野 [[思子淵神社 (高島市安曇川町中野)|思子淵神社]] === <gallery> ファイル:7-shicobuchi.JPG|思子淵神社 参道 </gallery> 滋賀県高島市安曇川町中野に鎮座。
== 七シコブチ以外の思子淵社 ==
; 朽木宮前坊 思子渕大明神 :邇々杵神社（ににぎじんじゃ）の境内社として祀られている。邇々杵神社本殿と河内神社本殿の間の奥に三つ並んだ祠のどれか。かつては別の場所にあったものをここに遷されたのだろう。
; 葛川細川 シコブチ神社 :安曇川右岸の八幡谷を少し上がった山の斜面に鎮座する八幡神社の境内社として祀られている。かつては別の場所にあったものをここに遷されたのだろう。
; 葛川町居 思子渕神社 :葛川左岸 町居の集落を北に抜けたところにある寺の奥の山の斜面に鎮座。
; 大原百井の思子淵神社 :京都府京都市左京区[[大原 (京都市)|大原]]百井町。百井川左岸の急な山の斜面に社や舞殿がある。林業関係者の崇敬を集め、樹皮を残したままの[[北山杉]]を用いた鳥居が建つ。 <gallery> ファイル:思古淵神社.jpg|思古淵神社 </gallery>
; 大原大見の思子淵神社 :京都府京都市左京区大原大見町。大見川源流にあって、参道と本殿の間を川が流れる（シコブチ神社としては）珍しい立地。 :2013年9月の台風18号により倒壊し、2018年4月に再建。その際に見つかった棟札から倒壊前の社は室町時代（1386年）に創建され、江戸時代（1725年）に再建されたことがわかった。（朝日新聞デジタル「（京の隠れ里に住んで）神社再建、新たな物語の始まり」2018年5月12日）
; 大原尾越の思子淵神社 :京都府京都市左京区大原尾越町。芦火谷川左岸の山の斜面に鎮座。祠は倒壊し灯籠のみ現存。
== ガワタロウ伝説 == 昔々、シコブチ明神は葛川梅ノ木付近の遅越の続きヶ原で我が子と一緒に筏を作り一緒に安曇川を下っていると突然筏が止まった。原因はなんなんだろうかと調べているとどうやら金山淵というところにある大きな岩に筏が衝突して動かなくなっているのがわかった。そして一緒に乗っていた子供の姿がなくなっていた、驚いた明神が竿を水中へ突き刺し辺りを探すと淵の底に河童が隠れており子供と一緒にいるのがわかった。明神はすぐさま河童を捕らえ子供を取り戻して河童をきつく叱った、だが中野の赤壁というところまで来るとまた河童は出現し筏の邪魔をした。明神はこのことに大変な怒りを覚え「今後どのような事があっても菅の蓑笠・がまの脚絆をつけ、拳の竿を持つものを妨害すればただじゃおかない」と言って叱りつけた。 ちなみにシコブチ明神は地主神だったとされる
== 関連項目 == * [[河童]]
== 外部リンク == * [http://www.pref.shiga.lg.jp/minwa/54/54-butai.html 民話でたどる滋賀の風景「しこぶちさん」]（音声あり） ** [http://www.pref.shiga.lg.jp/minwa/54/54-01.html しこぶち神社]
{{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:ななしこふち}} [[Category:神社 (祭神・信仰別)]] [[Category:京都市の神社|*ななしこふち]] [[Category:大津市の神社|*ななしこふち]] [[Category:高島市の神社|*ななしこふち]] [[Category:国津神]] [[Category:日本の神]] [[Category:日本の名数7|しこふち]] [[Category:河童]]

revision.comment: Bot: restore translated version
revision.text:
'''Seven Shikobuchi''' (七シコブチ) is a collective term for the seven major shrines dedicated to the deity '''Shikobuchi-kami''', a faith unique to the {{ill|Azumi River|ja|安曇川|qq=draft|qq=draft}} basin flowing through {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Shiga Prefecture|ja|滋賀県|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Legends of the ''Gawatarō'' ({{ill|Kappa|ja|河童|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) remain in all seven shrines. The name "Shikobuchi" is written in various forms, including 思子淵, 志古渕, and 信興淵.
== The Seven Shikobuchi Shrines == === Kuta {{ill|Shikobuchi Shrine|ja|思古渕社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Shikobuchi Jinja) === <gallery> File:1-shicobuchi.JPG|Shikobuchi Shrine, front view </gallery> Located in {{ill|Kuta|ja|久多|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Sakyō-ku|ja|左京区|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, this shrine is home to the ''Hanagasa-odori'' dance, which is designated as an intangible cultural asset.
=== Ogawa {{ill|Shishibuchi Shrine|ja|思子淵神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} === <gallery> File:2-shicobuchi.JPG|Shishibuchi Shrine, front view </gallery> Located in Kutsuki Ogawa, {{ill|Takashima|ja|高島市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Shiga Prefecture. A religious gathering called ''Shishibuchi Kō'' was held annually, where participants selected a host on a rotating basis, hung a sacred scroll inscribed with "Shishibuchi Shrine" in the alcove, made offerings, and prepared red rice for a communal meal. The shrine also possesses a copy of the ''Daihannya Sutra'', both designated as cultural assets.
=== Sakashita {{ill|Shishibuchi Daimyōjin|ja|思子渕大明神|qq=draft|qq=draft}} === <gallery> File:3-shicobuchi.JPG|Shishibuchi Daimyōjin, front view </gallery> Located in Katsuragawa Sakashita, {{ill|Ōtsu|ja|大津市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Shiga Prefecture. The shrine is a small sanctuary built atop a large boulder.
=== Bōmura {{ill|Shinkōbuchi Daimyōjin|ja|信興淵大明神|qq=draft|qq=draft}} === <gallery> File:4-shicobuchi.JPG|Katsuragawa Jinushi Shrine main hall & auxiliary shrine (right side) </gallery> Located in Katsuragawa Bōmura, Ōtsu, Shiga Prefecture. Enshrined as an auxiliary shrine within {{ill|Katsuragawa Jinushi Shrine|ja|葛川地主神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, it is associated with the founding of {{ill|Katsuragawa Myōō-in|ja|葛川明王院|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
=== Umenoki {{ill|Shikobuchi Shrine|ja|志子渕神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} === <gallery> File:5-shicobuchi.JPG|Shikobuchi Shrine </gallery> Located in Umenoki-chō, Ōtsu, Shiga Prefecture. Until recent times, its festival was held on the 7th day of the 10th lunar month.
=== Iwase {{ill|Shikobuchi Shrine|ja|志子淵神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} === <gallery> File:6-shicobuchi.JPG|Shikobuchi Shrine, main hall </gallery> Located in Kutsuki Iwase, Takashima, Shiga Prefecture. Shikobuchi-kami is revered as the "God of Rafting," and an annual ''Shikobuchi Kō'' religious gathering is held.
=== Nakano {{ill|Shishibuchi Shrine (Takashima, Azumi River Town, Nakano)|ja|思子淵神社 (高島市安曇川町中野)|qq=draft|qq=draft}} === <gallery> File:7-shicobuchi.JPG|Shishibuchi Shrine, approach path </gallery> Located in Azumi River Town, Nakano, Takashima, Shiga Prefecture.
== Other Shishibuchi Shrines == ; Kutsuki Miyamaebō {{ill|Shishibuchi Daimyōjin|ja|思子渕大明神|qq=draft|qq=draft}} : Enshrined within {{ill|Ninigi Shrine|ja|邇々杵神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Located between the main halls of Ninigi Shrine and {{ill|Kawachi Shrine|ja|河内神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, three small shrines stand together. It is believed to have been relocated from another site.
; Katsuragawa Hosokawa {{ill|Shikobuchi Shrine|ja|シコブチ神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} : An auxiliary shrine of the Hachiman Shrine, situated on a mountain slope along the right bank of the Azumi River. It is believed to have been relocated from another site.
; Katsuragawa Machii {{ill|Shishibuchi Shrine|ja|思子渕神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} : Located north of the Machii village settlement along the left bank of the Katsuragawa River, on a mountain slope behind a temple.
; Ōhara Momoi {{ill|Shishibuchi Shrine|ja|思子淵神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} : Located in {{ill|Ōhara, Kyoto|ja|大原 (京都市)|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Momoi-chō, Sakyō-ku, Kyoto. The shrine and a stage hall stand on a steep slope along the left bank of the Momoi River. Revered by forestry workers, its torii gate is constructed from unstripped {{ill|Kitayama cedar|ja|北山杉|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. <gallery> File:思古淵神社.jpg|Shikobuchi Shrine </gallery>
; Ōhara Ōmi {{ill|Shishibuchi Shrine|ja|思子淵神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} : Located in Ōhara Ōmi-chō, Sakyō-ku, Kyoto. Uniquely situated at the source of the Ōmi River, with a river flowing between its approach path and main hall. : Destroyed by Typhoon No. 18 in September 2013, it was rebuilt in April 2018. A discovered ridge plaque revealed that the previous shrine was originally founded in the Muromachi period (1386) and rebuilt in the Edo period (1725). (''Asahi Shimbun Digital'', May 12, 2018: "Rebuilding the Shrine in Kyoto's Hidden Village: A New Beginning")
; Ōhara Ogoshi {{ill|Shishibuchi Shrine|ja|思子淵神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} : Located in Ōhara Ogoshi-chō, Sakyō-ku, Kyoto, on a mountain slope along the left bank of the Ashibiyadani River. The shrine building has collapsed, leaving only the lanterns.
== The Gawatarō Legend == Long ago, Shikobuchi Myōjin and his child were rafting down the Azumi River from Tsuzukigahara in Katsuragawa Umenoki when their raft suddenly stopped. Upon investigating, they discovered that a large rock in Kanayama-buchi had blocked the raft's movement. At the same time, Shikobuchi Myōjin's child had vanished. Shocked, Myōjin plunged his pole into the water and searched the depths, finding a kappa hiding at the bottom with the child. Myōjin immediately captured the kappa, rescued his child, and scolded the kappa harshly. However, when they reached ''Aka-kabe'' in Nakano, the kappa reappeared and disrupted the raft again. Enraged, Myōjin warned the kappa, "From now on, if you ever interfere with someone wearing a ''suge no minokasa'' (straw raincoat) and ''gama no kyahan'' (rush leggings) while carrying a ''kobushi'' pole, you will face severe punishment!" It is believed that Shikobuchi Myōjin was originally a local land deity. == See Also == * {{ill|Kappa|ja|河童|qq=draft|qq=draft}}
== External Links == * [http://www.pref.shiga.lg.jp/minwa/54/54-butai.html Tracing Shiga’s Landscape Through Folktales: "Shikobuchi-san"] (Includes audio) ** [http://www.pref.shiga.lg.jp/minwa/54/54-01.html Shikobuchi Shrine]

revision.text:
'''Seven Shikobuchi''' (七シコブチ) is a collective term for the seven major shrines dedicated to the deity '''Shikobuchi-kami''', a faith unique to the {{ill|Azumi River|ja|安曇川|qq=draft|qq=draft}} basin flowing through {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Shiga Prefecture|ja|滋賀県|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Legends of the ''Gawatarō'' ({{ill|Kappa|ja|河童|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) remain in all seven shrines. The name "Shikobuchi" is written in various forms, including 思子淵, 志古渕, and 信興淵.
== The Seven Shikobuchi Shrines == === Kuta {{ill|Shikobuchi Shrine|ja|思古渕社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Shikobuchi Jinja) === <gallery> File:1-shicobuchi.JPG|Shikobuchi Shrine, front view </gallery> Located in {{ill|Kuta|ja|久多|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Sakyō-ku|ja|左京区|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, this shrine is home to the ''Hanagasa-odori'' dance, which is designated as an intangible cultural asset.
=== Ogawa {{ill|Shishibuchi Shrine|ja|思子淵神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} === <gallery> File:2-shicobuchi.JPG|Shishibuchi Shrine, front view </gallery> Located in Kutsuki Ogawa, {{ill|Takashima|ja|高島市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Shiga Prefecture. A religious gathering called ''Shishibuchi Kō'' was held annually, where participants selected a host on a rotating basis, hung a sacred scroll inscribed with "Shishibuchi Shrine" in the alcove, made offerings, and prepared red rice for a communal meal. The shrine also possesses a copy of the ''Daihannya Sutra'', both designated as cultural assets.
=== Sakashita {{ill|Shishibuchi Daimyōjin|ja|思子渕大明神|qq=draft|qq=draft}} === <gallery> File:3-shicobuchi.JPG|Shishibuchi Daimyōjin, front view </gallery> Located in Katsuragawa Sakashita, {{ill|Ōtsu|ja|大津市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Shiga Prefecture. The shrine is a small sanctuary built atop a large boulder.
=== Bōmura {{ill|Shinkōbuchi Daimyōjin|ja|信興淵大明神|qq=draft|qq=draft}} === <gallery> File:4-shicobuchi.JPG|Katsuragawa Jinushi Shrine main hall & auxiliary shrine (right side) </gallery> Located in Katsuragawa Bōmura, Ōtsu, Shiga Prefecture. Enshrined as an auxiliary shrine within {{ill|Katsuragawa Jinushi Shrine|ja|葛川地主神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, it is associated with the founding of {{ill|Katsuragawa Myōō-in|ja|葛川明王院|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
=== Umenoki {{ill|Shikobuchi Shrine|ja|志子渕神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} === <gallery> File:5-shicobuchi.JPG|Shikobuchi Shrine </gallery> Located in Umenoki-chō, Ōtsu, Shiga Prefecture. Until recent times, its festival was held on the 7th day of the 10th lunar month.
=== Iwase {{ill|Shikobuchi Shrine|ja|志子淵神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} === <gallery> File:6-shicobuchi.JPG|Shikobuchi Shrine, main hall </gallery> Located in Kutsuki Iwase, Takashima, Shiga Prefecture. Shikobuchi-kami is revered as the "God of Rafting," and an annual ''Shikobuchi Kō'' religious gathering is held.
=== Nakano {{ill|Shishibuchi Shrine (Takashima, Azumi River Town, Nakano)|ja|思子淵神社 (高島市安曇川町中野)|qq=draft|qq=draft}} === <gallery> File:7-shicobuchi.JPG|Shishibuchi Shrine, approach path </gallery> Located in Azumi River Town, Nakano, Takashima, Shiga Prefecture.
== Other Shishibuchi Shrines == ; Kutsuki Miyamaebō {{ill|Shishibuchi Daimyōjin|ja|思子渕大明神|qq=draft|qq=draft}} : Enshrined within {{ill|Ninigi Shrine|ja|邇々杵神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Located between the main halls of Ninigi Shrine and {{ill|Kawachi Shrine|ja|河内神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, three small shrines stand together. It is believed to have been relocated from another site.
; Katsuragawa Hosokawa {{ill|Shikobuchi Shrine|ja|シコブチ神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} : An auxiliary shrine of the Hachiman Shrine, situated on a mountain slope along the right bank of the Azumi River. It is believed to have been relocated from another site.
; Katsuragawa Machii {{ill|Shishibuchi Shrine|ja|思子渕神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} : Located north of the Machii village settlement along the left bank of the Katsuragawa River, on a mountain slope behind a temple.
; Ōhara Momoi {{ill|Shishibuchi Shrine|ja|思子淵神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} : Located in {{ill|Ōhara, Kyoto|ja|大原 (京都市)|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Momoi-chō, Sakyō-ku, Kyoto. The shrine and a stage hall stand on a steep slope along the left bank of the Momoi River. Revered by forestry workers, its torii gate is constructed from unstripped {{ill|Kitayama cedar|ja|北山杉|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. <gallery> File:思古淵神社.jpg|Shikobuchi Shrine </gallery>
; Ōhara Ōmi {{ill|Shishibuchi Shrine|ja|思子淵神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} : Located in Ōhara Ōmi-chō, Sakyō-ku, Kyoto. Uniquely situated at the source of the Ōmi River, with a river flowing between its approach path and main hall. : Destroyed by Typhoon No. 18 in September 2013, it was rebuilt in April 2018. A discovered ridge plaque revealed that the previous shrine was originally founded in the Muromachi period (1386) and rebuilt in the Edo period (1725). (''Asahi Shimbun Digital'', May 12, 2018: "Rebuilding the Shrine in Kyoto's Hidden Village: A New Beginning")
; Ōhara Ogoshi {{ill|Shishibuchi Shrine|ja|思子淵神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} : Located in Ōhara Ogoshi-chō, Sakyō-ku, Kyoto, on a mountain slope along the left bank of the Ashibiyadani River. The shrine building has collapsed, leaving only the lanterns.
== The Gawatarō Legend == Long ago, Shikobuchi Myōjin and his child were rafting down the Azumi River from Tsuzukigahara in Katsuragawa Umenoki when their raft suddenly stopped. Upon investigating, they discovered that a large rock in Kanayama-buchi had blocked the raft's movement. At the same time, Shikobuchi Myōjin's child had vanished. Shocked, Myōjin plunged his pole into the water and searched the depths, finding a kappa hiding at the bottom with the child. Myōjin immediately captured the kappa, rescued his child, and scolded the kappa harshly. However, when they reached ''Aka-kabe'' in Nakano, the kappa reappeared and disrupted the raft again. Enraged, Myōjin warned the kappa, "From now on, if you ever interfere with someone wearing a ''suge no minokasa'' (straw raincoat) and ''gama no kyahan'' (rush leggings) while carrying a ''kobushi'' pole, you will face severe punishment!" It is believed that Shikobuchi Myōjin was originally a local land deity. == See Also == * {{ill|Kappa|ja|河童|qq=draft|qq=draft}}
== External Links == * [http://www.pref.shiga.lg.jp/minwa/54/54-butai.html Tracing Shiga’s Landscape Through Folktales: "Shikobuchi-san"] (Includes audio) ** [http://www.pref.shiga.lg.jp/minwa/54/54-01.html Shikobuchi Shrine]
{{Shinto horizontal}} {{shinto-stub}}

revision.text:
'''Seven Shikobuchi''' (七シコブチ) is a collective term for the seven major shrines dedicated to the deity '''Shikobuchi-kami''', a faith unique to the {{ill|Azumi River|ja|安曇川|qq=draft|qq=draft}} basin flowing through {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Shiga Prefecture|ja|滋賀県|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Legends of the ''Gawatarō'' ({{ill|Kappa|ja|河童|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) remain in all seven shrines. The name "Shikobuchi" is written in various forms, including 思子淵, 志古渕, and 信興淵.
== The Seven Shikobuchi Shrines == === Kuta {{ill|Shikobuchi Shrine|ja|思古渕社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Shikobuchi Jinja) === <gallery> File:1-shicobuchi.JPG|Shikobuchi Shrine, front view </gallery> Located in {{ill|Kuta|ja|久多|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Sakyō-ku|ja|左京区|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, this shrine is home to the ''Hanagasa-odori'' dance, which is designated as an intangible cultural asset.
=== Ogawa {{ill|Shishibuchi Shrine|ja|思子淵神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} === <gallery> File:2-shicobuchi.JPG|Shishibuchi Shrine, front view </gallery> Located in Kutsuki Ogawa, {{ill|Takashima|ja|高島市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Shiga Prefecture. A religious gathering called ''Shishibuchi Kō'' was held annually, where participants selected a host on a rotating basis, hung a sacred scroll inscribed with "Shishibuchi Shrine" in the alcove, made offerings, and prepared red rice for a communal meal. The shrine also possesses a copy of the ''Daihannya Sutra'', both designated as cultural assets.
=== Sakashita {{ill|Shishibuchi Daimyōjin|ja|思子渕大明神|qq=draft|qq=draft}} === <gallery> File:3-shicobuchi.JPG|Shishibuchi Daimyōjin, front view </gallery> Located in Katsuragawa Sakashita, {{ill|Ōtsu|ja|大津市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Shiga Prefecture. The shrine is a small sanctuary built atop a large boulder.
=== Bōmura {{ill|Shinkōbuchi Daimyōjin|ja|信興淵大明神|qq=draft|qq=draft}} === <gallery> File:4-shicobuchi.JPG|Katsuragawa Jinushi Shrine main hall & auxiliary shrine (right side) </gallery> Located in Katsuragawa Bōmura, Ōtsu, Shiga Prefecture. Enshrined as an auxiliary shrine within {{ill|Katsuragawa Jinushi Shrine|ja|葛川地主神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, it is associated with the founding of {{ill|Katsuragawa Myōō-in|ja|葛川明王院|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
=== Umenoki {{ill|Shikobuchi Shrine|ja|志子渕神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} === <gallery> File:5-shicobuchi.JPG|Shikobuchi Shrine </gallery> Located in Umenoki-chō, Ōtsu, Shiga Prefecture. Until recent times, its festival was held on the 7th day of the 10th lunar month.
=== Iwase {{ill|Shikobuchi Shrine|ja|志子淵神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} === <gallery> File:6-shicobuchi.JPG|Shikobuchi Shrine, main hall </gallery> Located in Kutsuki Iwase, Takashima, Shiga Prefecture. Shikobuchi-kami is revered as the "God of Rafting," and an annual ''Shikobuchi Kō'' religious gathering is held.
=== Nakano {{ill|Shishibuchi Shrine (Takashima, Azumi River Town, Nakano)|ja|思子淵神社 (高島市安曇川町中野)|qq=draft|qq=draft}} === <gallery> File:7-shicobuchi.JPG|Shishibuchi Shrine, approach path </gallery> Located in Azumi River Town, Nakano, Takashima, Shiga Prefecture.
== Other Shishibuchi Shrines == ; Kutsuki Miyamaebō {{ill|Shishibuchi Daimyōjin|ja|思子渕大明神|qq=draft|qq=draft}} : Enshrined within {{ill|Ninigi Shrine|ja|邇々杵神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Located between the main halls of Ninigi Shrine and {{ill|Kawachi Shrine|ja|河内神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, three small shrines stand together. It is believed to have been relocated from another site.
; Katsuragawa Hosokawa {{ill|Shikobuchi Shrine|ja|シコブチ神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} : An auxiliary shrine of the Hachiman Shrine, situated on a mountain slope along the right bank of the Azumi River. It is believed to have been relocated from another site.
; Katsuragawa Machii {{ill|Shishibuchi Shrine|ja|思子渕神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} : Located north of the Machii village settlement along the left bank of the Katsuragawa River, on a mountain slope behind a temple.
; Ōhara Momoi {{ill|Shishibuchi Shrine|ja|思子淵神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} : Located in {{ill|Ōhara, Kyoto|ja|大原 (京都市)|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Momoi-chō, Sakyō-ku, Kyoto. The shrine and a stage hall stand on a steep slope along the left bank of the Momoi River. Revered by forestry workers, its torii gate is constructed from unstripped {{ill|Kitayama cedar|ja|北山杉|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. <gallery> File:思古淵神社.jpg|Shikobuchi Shrine </gallery>
; Ōhara Ōmi {{ill|Shishibuchi Shrine|ja|思子淵神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} : Located in Ōhara Ōmi-chō, Sakyō-ku, Kyoto. Uniquely situated at the source of the Ōmi River, with a river flowing between its approach path and main hall. : Destroyed by Typhoon No. 18 in September 2013, it was rebuilt in April 2018. A discovered ridge plaque revealed that the previous shrine was originally founded in the Muromachi period (1386) and rebuilt in the Edo period (1725). (''Asahi Shimbun Digital'', May 12, 2018: "Rebuilding the Shrine in Kyoto's Hidden Village: A New Beginning")
; Ōhara Ogoshi {{ill|Shishibuchi Shrine|ja|思子淵神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} : Located in Ōhara Ogoshi-chō, Sakyō-ku, Kyoto, on a mountain slope along the left bank of the Ashibiyadani River. The shrine building has collapsed, leaving only the lanterns.
== The Gawatarō Legend == Long ago, Shikobuchi Myōjin and his child were rafting down the Azumi River from Tsuzukigahara in Katsuragawa Umenoki when their raft suddenly stopped. Upon investigating, they discovered that a large rock in Kanayama-buchi had blocked the raft's movement. At the same time, Shikobuchi Myōjin's child had vanished. Shocked, Myōjin plunged his pole into the water and searched the depths, finding a kappa hiding at the bottom with the child. Myōjin immediately captured the kappa, rescued his child, and scolded the kappa harshly. However, when they reached ''Aka-kabe'' in Nakano, the kappa reappeared and disrupted the raft again. Enraged, Myōjin warned the kappa, "From now on, if you ever interfere with someone wearing a ''suge no minokasa'' (straw raincoat) and ''gama no kyahan'' (rush leggings) while carrying a ''kobushi'' pole, you will face severe punishment!" It is believed that Shikobuchi Myōjin was originally a local land deity. == See Also == * {{ill|Kappa|ja|河童|qq=draft|qq=draft}}
== External Links == * [http://www.pref.shiga.lg.jp/minwa/54/54-butai.html Tracing Shiga’s Landscape Through Folktales: "Shikobuchi-san"] (Includes audio) ** [http://www.pref.shiga.lg.jp/minwa/54/54-01.html Shikobuchi Shrine]
{{Shinto horizontal}} {{shinto-stub}}

'''Seven Shikobuchi''' (七シコブチ) is a collective term for the seven major shrines dedicated to the deity '''Shikobuchi-kami''', a faith unique to the {{ill|Azumi River|ja|安曇川|qq=draft|qq=draft}} basin flowing through {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Shiga Prefecture|ja|滋賀県|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Legends of the ''Gawatarō'' ({{ill|Kappa|ja|河童|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) remain in all seven shrines. The name "Shikobuchi" is written in various forms, including 思子淵, 志古渕, and 信興淵.
== The Seven Shikobuchi Shrines == === Kuta {{ill|Shikobuchi Shrine|ja|思古渕社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Shikobuchi Jinja) === <gallery> File:1-shicobuchi.JPG|Shikobuchi Shrine, front view </gallery> Located in {{ill|Kuta|ja|久多|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Sakyō-ku|ja|左京区|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, this shrine is home to the ''Hanagasa-odori'' dance, which is designated as an intangible cultural asset.
=== Ogawa {{ill|Shishibuchi Shrine|ja|思子淵神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} === <gallery> File:2-shicobuchi.JPG|Shishibuchi Shrine, front view </gallery> Located in Kutsuki Ogawa, {{ill|Takashima|ja|高島市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Shiga Prefecture. A religious gathering called ''Shishibuchi Kō'' was held annually, where participants selected a host on a rotating basis, hung a sacred scroll inscribed with "Shishibuchi Shrine" in the alcove, made offerings, and prepared red rice for a communal meal. The shrine also possesses a copy of the ''Daihannya Sutra'', both designated as cultural assets.
=== Sakashita {{ill|Shishibuchi Daimyōjin|ja|思子渕大明神|qq=draft|qq=draft}} === <gallery> File:3-shicobuchi.JPG|Shishibuchi Daimyōjin, front view </gallery> Located in Katsuragawa Sakashita, {{ill|Ōtsu|ja|大津市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Shiga Prefecture. The shrine is a small sanctuary built atop a large boulder.
=== Bōmura {{ill|Shinkōbuchi Daimyōjin|ja|信興淵大明神|qq=draft|qq=draft}} === <gallery> File:4-shicobuchi.JPG|Katsuragawa Jinushi Shrine main hall & auxiliary shrine (right side) </gallery> Located in Katsuragawa Bōmura, Ōtsu, Shiga Prefecture. Enshrined as an auxiliary shrine within {{ill|Katsuragawa Jinushi Shrine|ja|葛川地主神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, it is associated with the founding of {{ill|Katsuragawa Myōō-in|ja|葛川明王院|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
=== Umenoki {{ill|Shikobuchi Shrine|ja|志子渕神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} === <gallery> File:5-shicobuchi.JPG|Shikobuchi Shrine </gallery> Located in Umenoki-chō, Ōtsu, Shiga Prefecture. Until recent times, its festival was held on the 7th day of the 10th lunar month.
=== Iwase {{ill|Shikobuchi Shrine|ja|志子淵神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} === <gallery> File:6-shicobuchi.JPG|Shikobuchi Shrine, main hall </gallery> Located in Kutsuki Iwase, Takashima, Shiga Prefecture. Shikobuchi-kami is revered as the "God of Rafting," and an annual ''Shikobuchi Kō'' religious gathering is held.
=== Nakano {{ill|Shishibuchi Shrine (Takashima, Azumi River Town, Nakano)|ja|思子淵神社 (高島市安曇川町中野)|qq=draft|qq=draft}} === <gallery> File:7-shicobuchi.JPG|Shishibuchi Shrine, approach path </gallery> Located in Azumi River Town, Nakano, Takashima, Shiga Prefecture.
== Other Shishibuchi Shrines == ; Kutsuki Miyamaebō {{ill|Shishibuchi Daimyōjin|ja|思子渕大明神|qq=draft|qq=draft}} : Enshrined within {{ill|Ninigi Shrine|ja|邇々杵神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Located between the main halls of Ninigi Shrine and {{ill|Kawachi Shrine|ja|河内神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, three small shrines stand together. It is believed to have been relocated from another site.
; Katsuragawa Hosokawa {{ill|Shikobuchi Shrine|ja|シコブチ神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} : An auxiliary shrine of the Hachiman Shrine, situated on a mountain slope along the right bank of the Azumi River. It is believed to have been relocated from another site.
; Katsuragawa Machii {{ill|Shishibuchi Shrine|ja|思子渕神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} : Located north of the Machii village settlement along the left bank of the Katsuragawa River, on a mountain slope behind a temple.
; Ōhara Momoi {{ill|Shishibuchi Shrine|ja|思子淵神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} : Located in {{ill|Ōhara, Kyoto|ja|大原 (京都市)|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Momoi-chō, Sakyō-ku, Kyoto. The shrine and a stage hall stand on a steep slope along the left bank of the Momoi River. Revered by forestry workers, its torii gate is constructed from unstripped {{ill|Kitayama cedar|ja|北山杉|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. <gallery> File:思古淵神社.jpg|Shikobuchi Shrine </gallery>
; Ōhara Ōmi {{ill|Shishibuchi Shrine|ja|思子淵神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} : Located in Ōhara Ōmi-chō, Sakyō-ku, Kyoto. Uniquely situated at the source of the Ōmi River, with a river flowing between its approach path and main hall. : Destroyed by Typhoon No. 18 in September 2013, it was rebuilt in April 2018. A discovered ridge plaque revealed that the previous shrine was originally founded in the Muromachi period (1386) and rebuilt in the Edo period (1725). (''Asahi Shimbun Digital'', May 12, 2018: "Rebuilding the Shrine in Kyoto's Hidden Village: A New Beginning")
; Ōhara Ogoshi {{ill|Shishibuchi Shrine|ja|思子淵神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} : Located in Ōhara Ogoshi-chō, Sakyō-ku, Kyoto, on a mountain slope along the left bank of the Ashibiyadani River. The shrine building has collapsed, leaving only the lanterns.
== The Gawatarō Legend == Long ago, Shikobuchi Myōjin and his child were rafting down the Azumi River from Tsuzukigahara in Katsuragawa Umenoki when their raft suddenly stopped. Upon investigating, they discovered that a large rock in Kanayama-buchi had blocked the raft's movement. At the same time, Shikobuchi Myōjin's child had vanished. Shocked, Myōjin plunged his pole into the water and searched the depths, finding a kappa hiding at the bottom with the child. Myōjin immediately captured the kappa, rescued his child, and scolded the kappa harshly. However, when they reached ''Aka-kabe'' in Nakano, the kappa reappeared and disrupted the raft again. Enraged, Myōjin warned the kappa, "From now on, if you ever interfere with someone wearing a ''suge no minokasa'' (straw raincoat) and ''gama no kyahan'' (rush leggings) while carrying a ''kobushi'' pole, you will face severe punishment!" It is believed that Shikobuchi Myōjin was originally a local land deity. == See Also == * {{ill|Kappa|ja|河童|qq=draft|qq=draft}}
== External Links == * [http://www.pref.shiga.lg.jp/minwa/54/54-butai.html Tracing Shiga’s Landscape Through Folktales: "Shikobuchi-san"] (Includes audio) ** [http://www.pref.shiga.lg.jp/minwa/54/54-01.html Shikobuchi Shrine]

=== Ogawa {{ill|Shishibuchi Shrine|ja|思子淵神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} === <gallery> File:2-shicobuchi.JPG|Shishibuchi Shrine, front view </gallery> Located in Kutsuki Ogawa, {{ill|Takashima|ja|高島市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Shiga Prefecture. A religious gathering called ''Shishibuchi Kō'' was held annually, where participants selected a host on a rotating basis, hung a sacred scroll inscribed with "Shishibuchi Shrine" in the alcove, made offerings, and prepared red rice for a communal meal. The shrine also possesses a copy of the ''Daihannya Sutra'', both designated as cultural assets.
=== Sakashita {{ill|Shishibuchi Daimyōjin|ja|思子渕大明神|qq=draft|qq=draft}} === <gallery> File:3-shicobuchi.JPG|Shishibuchi Daimyōjin, front view </gallery> Located in Katsuragawa Sakashita, {{ill|Ōtsu|ja|大津市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Shiga Prefecture. The shrine is a small sanctuary built atop a large boulder.
=== Bōmura {{ill|Shinkōbuchi Daimyōjin|ja|信興淵大明神|qq=draft|qq=draft}} === <gallery> File:4-shicobuchi.JPG|Katsuragawa Jinushi Shrine main hall & auxiliary shrine (right side) </gallery> Located in Katsuragawa Bōmura, Ōtsu, Shiga Prefecture. Enshrined as an auxiliary shrine within {{ill|Katsuragawa Jinushi Shrine|ja|葛川地主神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, it is associated with the founding of {{ill|Katsuragawa Myōō-in|ja|葛川明王院|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
=== Umenoki {{ill|Shikobuchi Shrine|ja|志子渕神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} === <gallery> File:5-shicobuchi.JPG|Shikobuchi Shrine </gallery> Located in Umenoki-chō, Ōtsu, Shiga Prefecture. Until recent times, its festival was held on the 7th day of the 10th lunar month.
=== Iwase {{ill|Shikobuchi Shrine|ja|志子淵神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} === <gallery> File:6-shicobuchi.JPG|Shikobuchi Shrine, main hall </gallery> Located in Kutsuki Iwase, Takashima, Shiga Prefecture. Shikobuchi-kami is revered as the "God of Rafting," and an annual ''Shikobuchi Kō'' religious gathering is held.
=== Nakano {{ill|Shishibuchi Shrine (Takashima, Azumi River Town, Nakano)|ja|思子淵神社 (高島市安曇川町中野)|qq=draft|qq=draft}} === <gallery> File:7-shicobuchi.JPG|Shishibuchi Shrine, approach path </gallery> Located in Azumi River Town, Nakano, Takashima, Shiga Prefecture.
== Other Shishibuchi Shrines == ; Kutsuki Miyamaebō {{ill|Shishibuchi Daimyōjin|ja|思子渕大明神|qq=draft|qq=draft}} : Enshrined within {{ill|Ninigi Shrine|ja|邇々杵神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Located between the main halls of Ninigi Shrine and {{ill|Kawachi Shrine|ja|河内神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, three small shrines stand together. It is believed to have been relocated from another site.
; Katsuragawa Hosokawa {{ill|Shikobuchi Shrine|ja|シコブチ神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} : An auxiliary shrine of the Hachiman Shrine, situated on a mountain slope along the right bank of the Azumi River. It is believed to have been relocated from another site.
; Katsuragawa Machii {{ill|Shishibuchi Shrine|ja|思子渕神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} : Located north of the Machii village settlement along the left bank of the Katsuragawa River, on a mountain slope behind a temple.
; Ōhara Momoi {{ill|Shishibuchi Shrine|ja|思子淵神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} : Located in {{ill|Ōhara, Kyoto|ja|大原 (京都市)|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Momoi-chō, Sakyō-ku, Kyoto. The shrine and a stage hall stand on a steep slope along the left bank of the Momoi River. Revered by forestry workers, its torii gate is constructed from unstripped {{ill|Kitayama cedar|ja|北山杉|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. <gallery> File:思古淵神社.jpg|Shikobuchi Shrine </gallery>
; Ōhara Ōmi {{ill|Shishibuchi Shrine|ja|思子淵神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} : Located in Ōhara Ōmi-chō, Sakyō-ku, Kyoto. Uniquely situated at the source of the Ōmi River, with a river flowing between its approach path and main hall. : Destroyed by Typhoon No. 18 in September 2013, it was rebuilt in April 2018. A discovered ridge plaque revealed that the previous shrine was originally founded in the Muromachi period (1386) and rebuilt in the Edo period (1725). (''Asahi Shimbun Digital'', May 12, 2018: "Rebuilding the Shrine in Kyoto's Hidden Village: A New Beginning")
; Ōhara Ogoshi {{ill|Shishibuchi Shrine|ja|思子淵神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} : Located in Ōhara Ogoshi-chō, Sakyō-ku, Kyoto, on a mountain slope along the left bank of the Ashibiyadani River. The shrine building has collapsed, leaving only the lanterns.
== The Gawatarō Legend == Long ago, Shikobuchi Myōjin and his child were rafting down the Azumi River from Tsuzukigahara in Katsuragawa Umenoki when their raft suddenly stopped. Upon investigating, they discovered that a large rock in Kanayama-buchi had blocked the raft's movement. At the same time, Shikobuchi Myōjin's child had vanished. Shocked, Myōjin plunged his pole into the water and searched the depths, finding a kappa hiding at the bottom with the child. Myōjin immediately captured the kappa, rescued his child, and scolded the kappa harshly. However, when they reached ''Aka-kabe'' in Nakano, the kappa reappeared and disrupted the raft again. Enraged, Myōjin warned the kappa, "From now on, if you ever interfere with someone wearing a ''suge no minokasa'' (straw raincoat) and ''gama no kyahan'' (rush leggings) while carrying a ''kobushi'' pole, you will face severe punishment!" It is believed that Shikobuchi Myōjin was originally a local land deity. == See Also == * {{ill|Kappa|ja|河童|qq=draft|qq=draft}}
== External Links == * [http://www.pref.shiga.lg.jp/minwa/54/54-butai.html Tracing Shiga’s Landscape Through Folktales: "Shikobuchi-san"] (Includes audio) ** [http://www.pref.shiga.lg.jp/minwa/54/54-01.html Shikobuchi Shrine]

revision.comment: Bot: remove [[Category:Kappa]] from page
revision.text:
'''Seven Shikobuchi''' (七シコブチ) is a collective term for the seven major shrines dedicated to the deity '''Shikobuchi-kami''', a faith unique to the {{ill|Azumi River|ja|安曇川|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Azumi River|qq=}} basin flowing through {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto Prefecture|qq=}} and {{ill|Shiga Prefecture|ja|滋賀県|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiga Prefecture|qq=}}. Legends of the ''Gawatarō'' ({{ill|Kappa|ja|河童|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kappa|qq=}}) remain in all seven shrines. The name "Shikobuchi" is written in various forms, including 思子淵, 志古渕, and 信興淵.
== The Seven Shikobuchi Shrines == === Kuta {{ill|Shikobuchi Shrine|ja|思古渕社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikobuchi Shrine|qq=}} (Shikobuchi Jinja) === <gallery> File:1-shicobuchi.JPG|Shikobuchi Shrine, front view </gallery> Located in {{ill|Kuta|ja|久多|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuta|qq=}}, {{ill|Sakyō-ku|ja|左京区|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakyō-ku|qq=}}, {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|qq=}}, this shrine is home to the ''Hanagasa-odori'' dance, which is designated as an intangible cultural asset.
=== Ogawa {{ill|Shishibuchi Shrine|ja|思子淵神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shishibuchi Shrine|qq=}} === <gallery> File:2-shicobuchi.JPG|Shishibuchi Shrine, front view </gallery> Located in Kutsuki Ogawa, {{ill|Takashima|ja|高島市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takashima|qq=}}, Shiga Prefecture. A religious gathering called ''Shishibuchi Kō'' was held annually, where participants selected a host on a rotating basis, hung a sacred scroll inscribed with "Shishibuchi Shrine" in the alcove, made offerings, and prepared red rice for a communal meal. The shrine also possesses a copy of the ''Daihannya Sutra'', both designated as cultural assets.
=== Sakashita {{ill|Shishibuchi Daimyōjin|ja|思子渕大明神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shishibuchi Daimyōjin|qq=}} === <gallery> File:3-shicobuchi.JPG|Shishibuchi Daimyōjin, front view </gallery> Located in Katsuragawa Sakashita, {{ill|Ōtsu|ja|大津市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtsu|qq=}}, Shiga Prefecture. The shrine is a small sanctuary built atop a large boulder.
=== Bōmura {{ill|Shinkōbuchi Daimyōjin|ja|信興淵大明神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinkōbuchi Daimyōjin|qq=}} === <gallery> File:4-shicobuchi.JPG|Katsuragawa Jinushi Shrine main hall & auxiliary shrine (right side) </gallery> Located in Katsuragawa Bōmura, Ōtsu, Shiga Prefecture. Enshrined as an auxiliary shrine within {{ill|Katsuragawa Jinushi Shrine|ja|葛川地主神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katsuragawa Jinushi Shrine|qq=}}, it is associated with the founding of {{ill|Katsuragawa Myōō-in|ja|葛川明王院|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katsuragawa Myōō-in|qq=}}.
=== Umenoki {{ill|Shikobuchi Shrine|ja|志子渕神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikobuchi Shrine|qq=}} === <gallery> File:5-shicobuchi.JPG|Shikobuchi Shrine </gallery> Located in Umenoki-chō, Ōtsu, Shiga Prefecture. Until recent times, its festival was held on the 7th day of the 10th lunar month.
=== Iwase {{ill|Shikobuchi Shrine|ja|志子淵神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikobuchi Shrine|qq=}} === <gallery> File:6-shicobuchi.JPG|Shikobuchi Shrine, main hall </gallery> Located in Kutsuki Iwase, Takashima, Shiga Prefecture. Shikobuchi-kami is revered as the "God of Rafting," and an annual ''Shikobuchi Kō'' religious gathering is held.
=== Nakano {{ill|Shishibuchi Shrine (Takashima, Azumi River Town, Nakano)|ja|思子淵神社 (高島市安曇川町中野)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shishibuchi Shrine (Takashima, Azumi River Town, Nakano)|qq=}} === <gallery> File:7-shicobuchi.JPG|Shishibuchi Shrine, approach path </gallery> Located in Azumi River Town, Nakano, Takashima, Shiga Prefecture.
== Other Shishibuchi Shrines == ; Kutsuki Miyamaebō {{ill|Shishibuchi Daimyōjin|ja|思子渕大明神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shishibuchi Daimyōjin|qq=}} : Enshrined within {{ill|Ninigi Shrine|ja|邇々杵神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninigi Shrine|qq=}}. Located between the main halls of Ninigi Shrine and {{ill|Kawachi Shrine|ja|河内神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawachi Shrine|qq=}}, three small shrines stand together. It is believed to have been relocated from another site.
; Katsuragawa Hosokawa {{ill|Shikobuchi Shrine|ja|シコブチ神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikobuchi Shrine|qq=}} : An auxiliary shrine of the Hachiman Shrine, situated on a mountain slope along the right bank of the Azumi River. It is believed to have been relocated from another site.
; Katsuragawa Machii {{ill|Shishibuchi Shrine|ja|思子渕神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shishibuchi Shrine|qq=}} : Located north of the Machii village settlement along the left bank of the Katsuragawa River, on a mountain slope behind a temple.
; Ōhara Momoi {{ill|Shishibuchi Shrine|ja|思子淵神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shishibuchi Shrine|qq=}} : Located in {{ill|Ōhara, Kyoto|ja|大原 (京都市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōhara, Kyoto|qq=}}, Momoi-chō, Sakyō-ku, Kyoto. The shrine and a stage hall stand on a steep slope along the left bank of the Momoi River. Revered by forestry workers, its torii gate is constructed from unstripped {{ill|Kitayama cedar|ja|北山杉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitayama cedar|qq=}}. <gallery> File:思古淵神社.jpg|Shikobuchi Shrine </gallery>
; Ōhara Ōmi {{ill|Shishibuchi Shrine|ja|思子淵神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shishibuchi Shrine|qq=}} : Located in Ōhara Ōmi-chō, Sakyō-ku, Kyoto. Uniquely situated at the source of the Ōmi River, with a river flowing between its approach path and main hall. : Destroyed by Typhoon No. 18 in September 2013, it was rebuilt in April 2018. A discovered ridge plaque revealed that the previous shrine was originally founded in the Muromachi period (1386) and rebuilt in the Edo period (1725). (''Asahi Shimbun Digital'', May 12, 2018: "Rebuilding the Shrine in Kyoto's Hidden Village: A New Beginning")
; Ōhara Ogoshi {{ill|Shishibuchi Shrine|ja|思子淵神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shishibuchi Shrine|qq=}} : Located in Ōhara Ogoshi-chō, Sakyō-ku, Kyoto, on a mountain slope along the left bank of the Ashibiyadani River. The shrine building has collapsed, leaving only the lanterns.
== The Gawatarō Legend == Long ago, Shikobuchi Myōjin and his child were rafting down the Azumi River from Tsuzukigahara in Katsuragawa Umenoki when their raft suddenly stopped. Upon investigating, they discovered that a large rock in Kanayama-buchi had blocked the raft's movement. At the same time, Shikobuchi Myōjin's child had vanished. Shocked, Myōjin plunged his pole into the water and searched the depths, finding a kappa hiding at the bottom with the child. Myōjin immediately captured the kappa, rescued his child, and scolded the kappa harshly. However, when they reached ''Aka-kabe'' in Nakano, the kappa reappeared and disrupted the raft again. Enraged, Myōjin warned the kappa, "From now on, if you ever interfere with someone wearing a ''suge no minokasa'' (straw raincoat) and ''gama no kyahan'' (rush leggings) while carrying a ''kobushi'' pole, you will face severe punishment!" It is believed that Shikobuchi Myōjin was originally a local land deity. == See Also == * {{ill|Kappa|ja|河童|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kappa|qq=}}
== External Links == * [http://www.pref.shiga.lg.jp/minwa/54/54-butai.html Tracing Shiga’s Landscape Through Folktales: "Shikobuchi-san"] (Includes audio) ** [http://www.pref.shiga.lg.jp/minwa/54/54-01.html Shikobuchi Shrine]
